en.extract:
The Galápagos penguin (Spheniscus mendiculus) is a penguin endemic to the Galápagos Islands of Ecuador. It is the only penguin found north of the equator. Most inhabit Fernandina Island and the west coast of Isabela Island. The cool waters of the Humboldt and Cromwell Currents allow it to survive despite the tropical latitude. The Galápagos penguin is one of the banded penguins, the other species of which live mostly on the coasts of Africa and mainland South America. Due to their warm environment, Galápagos penguins have developed techniques to stay cool. The feathers on their back, flippers, and head are black, and they have a white belly and a stripe looping from their eyes down to their neck and chin. Each penguin keeps only one mate, and breeds year-round. Because there are no soft areas to nest, their nests are typically in caves and crevices which also provide protection against predators and the harsh environment. The Galápagos penguin has a lifespan of 20 years, but due to predation, life expectancy in the wild could be significantly reduced. They have been critically impacted to the point of endangerment by climate change and pollution caused by plastic waste due to tourism and urbanization.


Description
The average Galápagos penguin is 48–50 centimetres (19–20 in) tall and weighs around 2–4 kilograms (4.4–8.8 lb). It is the second-smallest species of penguin, after the little penguin. Females are usually smaller than males. Galápagos penguins have a black head with a white border running from behind the eye, around the black ear coverts and chin, to join on the throat. The top of the beaks are black and fade into pink on the bottom. They have two black bands across the breast that connect to the back, the lower band extending down the flanks to the thigh. Juveniles differ in having a wholly dark head, grayer on side and chin, and no breast band.


History
Many years ago, the Humboldt Current is believed to have brought the penguins from mainland South America to the Galápagos Islands, where they have evolved in isolation into a separate species. Islands such as Isabela Island contained cold and nutrient-rich waters that provided abundant food, ensuring the survival and reproduction of penguins. Over millions of years, the penguins underwent adaptations, developing unique features that enabled their existence and allowed them to thrive under such an ecological niche. Today, the Galápagos penguins are among the smallest penguin species.


Distribution
Ninety percent of Galápagos penguins live on Fernandina Island and the west coast of Isabela Island, in the western part of the archipelago, but small populations also occur on Santiago, Bartolomé, northern Santa Cruz, and Floreana. The northern tip of Isabela crosses the equator, meaning that some Galápagos penguins live in the Northern Hemisphere, the only penguins to do so.


Behavior

Galápagos penguins are confined to the Galápagos Islands, foraging in the cool Cromwell Current during the day and returning to the land at night. They eat small schooling fish, mainly mullet, sardines, pilchards and anchovies, and sometimes crustaceans. They play a role in regulating the populations of the marine species they consume. At the same time, the adult Galápagos penguins are a food source for other predators such as sharks and whales. They normally range only a few kilometers from their breeding sites, depending on the cold, nutrient-rich currents to bring them food.
Air temperatures in the Galápagos remain in the range 15–28 °C (59–82 °F). During El Niño seasons, the penguins defer breeding because their food becomes less abundant; this makes the chances of raising offspring successfully unfavorable compared to the chances of dying in the attempt. This was especially detrimental during the 1982-83 El Niño, where a decline in population of 77% was observed. The penguins usually breed when the sea surface temperature is below 25 °C (77 °F). The strong tropical sun is problematic for this species. Their primary means of cooling off is going in the water, but other behavioral adaptations for thermoregulation come into play when they must remain on land. One method involves stretching out their flippers and hunching forward to keep the sun from shining on their feet, which exchange heat rapidly because they have high blood flow and lack insulation. Another method is to pant, using evaporation to cool the throat and airways. Galápagos penguins protect their eggs and chicks from the hot sun by keeping them in deep crevices in the rocks.

The Galápagos penguins' flipper-like wings and streamlined bodies enhance their easy movements in water. Their white and black colors also aid in thermal regulation and camouflaging. All these features promote and ensure survival in the harsh conditions present in their coastal habitat, hence the symbiotic relationship between the Galápagos penguins and their natural environment. The penguins' breeding patterns depend on finding suitable nesting places within the volcanic landscape. The Galápagos penguins find refuge in the cool and dark lava caves, which provide the appropriate conditions for raising their offspring since they offer protection from the sun, ensuring survival. This indicates specific behavioral patterns and features that enhance their adaptability in this particular environment.


Breeding and reproduction

Galápagos penguins are a monogamous species, each pair mating for life. There are around 1,000 breeding pairs of Galápagos penguins in the world, and breeding depends on certain climate events. During El Niño, birds may not breed due to these climate conditions. After completing courtship, with rituals including bill dueling, flipper patting, and mutual preening, the pair will build and maintain a nest. Most nests are seen between May and July because both quantity of food and climate conditions are typically most optimal. The nests are made within 50 metres (160 ft) of the water on the shore. Adults stay near the breeding area during the year with their mate. It lays one or two eggs in places such as caves and crevices, protected from direct sunlight, which can cause the eggs to overheat. Temperature of surface water also influences reproduction as they prefer temperatures between 17 and 22 °C (63 and 72 °F) to incubate, above 25 °C (77 °F) causes their nests to fail. Incubation takes 38–40 days, with both parents incubating.
One parent will always stay with the eggs or young chicks while the other may be absent for several days to feed. A pair usually rears only one chick. Galápagos penguins will molt before they breed, and are the only penguins to do this twice a year. Molting takes up to 15 days to complete. They do this for their own safety, as food availability in the Galápagos is typically unpredictable. If there is not enough food available, they may abandon the nest.
It takes about 60–65 days for the chicks to become independent. Newly hatched chicks have downy feathers that do not become waterproof until the chicks grow into juveniles. The juvenile plumage, attained by thirty days after a chick hatches, is dark brown or gray above and white below. These feathers are mainly needed to protect the chicks from the strong sun rather than keep them warm.
Bermudian naturalist Louis L. Mowbray was the first to successfully breed the Galápagos penguins in captivity.


Population

Galápagos penguins have a lifespan ranging from 15 to 20 years, but because of environmental factors and predation, their life expectancy is reduced. They are listed on the IUCN Red List of Threatened Species as Endangered.  Their mean population size from the years 1993–2003 was an average of 1,500. In 2003 there were an estimated 1,351 penguins and as of 2018 there are around 1,200 mature penguins left. The Galapagos penguin is currently one of the rarest penguin species, next to another penguin species, the yellow-eyed penguin.
The population size on average is about 1,500 individuals per year, depending on the climate events. Population trends were seen to increase and decrease in a span of a year due to La Niña and El Niño events, weather events that affect the conditions on the Galápagos Islands. After a La Niña event, both population size and reproduction were both seen to have an increase in number. El Niño events have been found to reduce Galápagos penguin populations, in some cases nearly as much as 50% decrease was seen in population size. This is due to the warmer climate disrupting the cold water that holds the nutrients that support the marine ecosystem.


Conservation


El Niño and climate change
The Galápagos penguin is a particularly vulnerable bird species due to its limited range on the Galápagos Islands. With a population of only about 1800, it remains on the endangered species list, and its population will likely fluctuate strongly in response to anthropogenic changes in the region. The primary danger to the Galápagos penguin is the climate phenomena known as El Niño. In 1982–83 and 1997–98, two strong El Niño events resulted in Galápagos penguin population declines of 77% and 65%, respectively. Additionally, the years 1965–66, 1968–69, 1972–73, 1976, 1986–87, 1991–92 and 1993 all had relatively weak El Niño events which were associated with slow population recovery. Conversely, during La Niña events when sea surface temperatures are lower than normal and the climate patterns shift, Galápagos penguin populations begin to recover. Anthropogenic climate change has been shown to increase frequency and intensity of El Niño events beyond levels of natural variability which negatively impacts the Galápagos penguin. Warmer temperatures caused by El Niño are associated with poorer female condition and lower adult body weights. Body conditions fluctuate along with the climate events, evidence showing that weight is enhanced during La Nina's cooler temperatures but body weight deteriorates during El Nino when surface level waters are warmed. Under severe conditions, the penguins can even suffer from starvation. This is due to the warmer temperatures of El Niño events resulting in a decrease in upwelling of the cold nutrient rich waters which decreases phytoplankton productivity and results in bottom up trophic disruptions that reduce the food availability for the Galápagos penguin. This lack of food leads to poor breeding success and a disproportionate female death rate, causing population decline and disrupting future recovery by creating uneven sex ratios in the populations. Predictive models suggest future El Niño events will increase in frequency and severity over the next century, posing further threats to the Galápagos penguin. If the pattern of El Niño events from 1965 to 2004 continues, there is a 30% chance of extinction for the Galápagos penguin. If the frequency of strong El Niño events were to double over this same time period, the chance of extinction would be 80%.


Disease
Another potential threat to the Galápagos penguin is disease. Preliminary studies, such as one conducted in 2001, found no evidence of avian malaria or Marek's disease in Galápagos penguin populations. Despite these findings, the researchers recommended further observations, citing the death of 800 chickens in the Galápagos Islands from Marek's disease, the presence of mosquitos known to carry Avian malaria, and the known impacts of these diseases on other endemic bird populations in Hawaii. Later, a 2009 study revealed the presence of a species of Plasmodium in Galápagos penguins, a genus of blood parasite that causes Avian malaria. The presence of this parasite suggests that diseases are able to travel from other populations to the isolated Galápagos penguin communities and is likely a cause for population decline. Further research suggests that cross-species transmission may occur between endemic Galápagos species and migratory birds such as the Bobolink. Understanding how these diseases reach the Galápagos Islands and transmit between its bird species is a focus for developing conservation strategies for endangered species such as the Galápagos penguin.


Other threats
Other threats include humans harvesting penguins for oil and other products, competition with fisheries for krill and other fish, habitat loss, increased predation from invasive species, bycatch, and pollution. On Isabela Island, humans may be contributing to the decline of this species due to introduced cats, dogs, and rats which attack penguins, destroy nests, and spread disease. Other threats on land include Sally lightfoot crabs, Galapagos snakes (Pseudalsophis slevini and Pseudalsophis dorsalis), Galapagos rice rats, Galápagos hawks, and short-eared owls. While in the water, predators include sharks, fur seals, and sea lions. They also face the hazards of unreliable food resources and volcanic activity. Illegal fishing activity may interrupt the penguins' nesting, and they are often caught as bycatch in fishing nets.
These impacts are particularly threatening because of the population structure of the Galápagos penguin. The Galápagos penguin consists of two geographic subpopulations, but studies suggest that there is sufficient gene flow between these populations to treat them together when considering conservation strategies. Additionally, the Galápagos penguin demonstrates relatively low genetic diversity, making it especially vulnerable to disease, predation, and other environmental changes.
As ecological tourism becomes more common on the Galápagos, it is having a more significant impact on native wildlife, including the Galápagos penguins. When incorrectly executed and poorly managed, tourism can contribute to reproductive failure, burrow collapse, or even death to these animals.
Galápagos penguins are protected under the Antarctic Treaty of 1959, which includes all 18 penguin species. Additionally, Galápagos penguins are protected under Ecuadorian jurisdiction; the Galápagos National Park and the Galápagos Marine Reserve are two important entities that are responsible for protecting the Galápagos islands' various species, including the Galápagos penguins. They were able to accomplish this through limiting human activity and interference from outside factors such as fishing and introducing new species to the island.


Ecology
The Galápagos penguin are unique to the Galápagos Islands, predominantly inhabiting Fernandina Island and Isabela Island. These islands offer an environment crucial for these birds' survival and proliferation.


Habitat and interaction with the environment
The Galápagos penguins have a symbiotic relationship with their habitat. Fernandina and Isabela Islands provide rocky coastlines ideal for nesting, and the surrounding waters, enriched by the Cromwell Current, abound with fish and crustaceans that form their diet. This feeding habit plays a pivotal role in the local marine ecosystem. They help balance the marine food web and contribute to nutrient cycling. The guano produced by these penguins is a rich fertilizer, enhancing the fertility of the coastal soil and indirectly supporting certain plant species native to the islands.


Adaptation to island conditions
Adapting to the equatorial climate of the Galápagos, these penguins exhibit unique behavioral and physiological traits. Unlike their Antarctic counterparts, they have evolved to withstand warmer temperatures. They pant and seek shade during the hottest parts of the day to regulate body temperature. Physiologically, they have a reduced layer of body fat and smaller size, aiding heat dissipation.
Galápagos penguins breed throughout the year, capitalizing on the fluctuating availability of food resources linked to ocean currents. This flexibility in breeding patterns is a critical adaptation for survival in an environment where food availability is inconsistent.


Impact of environmental factors on life expectancy and population
Environmental conditions significantly influence the life expectancy and population numbers of Galápagos penguins. Climate events like El Niño significantly affect the marine ecosystem, reducing food availability. During such events, penguin populations face starvation, reduced breeding success, and increased mortality. Furthermore, human-induced factors such as pollution, oil spills, and fishing activities pose additional threats to their survival.
Conservation efforts are crucial for protecting these penguins, which are classified as endangered by the IUCN Red List. Measures include monitoring population trends, habitat preservation, and mitigating human impacts. These efforts are essential to ensure the survival of the Galápagos penguin, a species integral to the biodiversity and ecological balance of the Galápagos Islands.


References


External links

Galápagos penguins from the International Penguin Conservation website
BirdLife species factsheet
Penguin World: Galápagos penguin

The '''Galápagos penguin''' ('''''Spheniscus mendiculus''''') is a [[penguin]] [[Endemism|endemic]] to the [[Galápagos Islands]] of [[Ecuador]]. It is the only penguin found north of the [[equator]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldwildlife.org/species/galapagos-penguin |title=World Wide Fund for Nature – Galapagos Penguin Facts |website=World Wide Fund for Nature}}</ref> Most inhabit [[Fernandina Island]] and the west coast of [[Isabela Island (Galápagos)|Isabela Island]].<ref name="Galapagos Conservation Trust" /> The cool waters of the [[Humboldt Current|Humboldt]] and [[Cromwell Current]]s allow it to survive despite the tropical latitude. The Galápagos penguin is one of the [[banded penguin]]s, the other species of which live mostly on the coasts of [[Africa]] and mainland [[South America]]. Due to their warm environment, Galápagos penguins have developed techniques to stay cool. The feathers on their back, flippers, and head are black, and they have a white belly and a stripe looping from their eyes down to their neck and chin. Each penguin keeps only one mate,<ref name="Galapagos Conservation Trust">{{cite web |title=Galapagos penguin |url=https://galapagosconservation.org.uk/species/galapagos-penguin/ |website=Galapagos Conservation Trust |access-date=13 June 2024}}</ref> and breeds year-round. Because there are no soft areas to nest, their nests are typically in caves and crevices<ref name="Galapagos Conservation Trust" /> which also provide protection against predators and the harsh environment. The Galápagos penguin has a lifespan of 20 years,<ref name="Galapagos Conservation Trust" /> but due to [[predation]], life expectancy in the wild could be significantly reduced. They have been critically impacted to the point of endangerment by [[climate change]] and pollution caused by [[plastic waste]] due to tourism and [[urbanization]].<ref>{{Citation |last1=Alava |first1=Juan José |title=Pollution as an Emerging Threat for the Conservation of the Galapagos Marine Reserve: Environmental Impacts and Management Perspectives |date=2014 |work=The Galapagos Marine Reserve: A Dynamic Social-Ecological System |pages=247–283 |editor-last=Denkinger |editor-first=Judith |series=Social and Ecological Interactions in the Galapagos Islands |place=Cham |publisher=Springer International Publishing |language=en |doi=10.1007/978-3-319-02769-2_12 |isbn=978-3-319-02769-2 |last2=Palomera |first2=Carlos |last3=Bendell |first3=Leah |last4=Ross |first4=Peter S. |editor2-last=Vinueza |editor2-first=Luis}}</ref>

==Description==
The average Galápagos penguin is {{convert|48|-|50|cm|in}} tall and weighs around {{convert|2|-|4|kg|lb}}.<ref name="AZ Animals">{{Cite web|title=Galapagos Penguin |url=https://a-z-animals.com/animals/galapagos-penguin/|access-date=9 November 2020|website=AZ Animals|date=10 September 2018 |language=en-US}}</ref> It is the second-smallest species of penguin, after the [[little penguin]]. Females are usually smaller than males. Galápagos penguins have a black head with a white border running from behind the eye, around the black ear coverts and chin, to join on the throat. The top of the beaks are black and fade into pink on the bottom.<ref name="Penguins-World-2013">{{Cite web|website=Penguins-World|title=Galapagos Penguin – Penguin Facts and Information|date=7 October 2013 |url=https://www.penguins-world.com/galapagos-penguin/ |access-date=21 October 2020 |language=en-US}}</ref> They have two black bands across the breast that connect to the back, the lower band extending down the flanks to the thigh.<ref name="Wahlstrom">{{Cite web|last=Wahlstrom |first=Joshua |title=Spheniscus mendiculus (Galapagos penguin)|url=https://animaldiversity.org/accounts/Spheniscus_mendiculus/ |access-date=20 October 2020 |website=Animal Diversity Web |language=en}}</ref> Juveniles differ in having a wholly dark head, grayer on side and chin, and no breast band.<ref name="Penguins-World-2013" />

== History ==
Many years ago,{{When|date=January 2026}} the [[Humboldt Current]] is believed to have brought the penguins from mainland [[South America]] to the [[Galápagos Islands]], where they have evolved in isolation into a separate species.<ref name="Loughlin-2022">{{Cite book |last1=Loughlin |first1=Kevin |title=Galápagos |last2=Kricher |first2=John C. |date=2022 |publisher=Princeton University Press |doi=10.1353/book.109379 |isbn=978-0-691-24253-8}}</ref> Islands such as [[Isabela Island (Galápagos)|Isabela Island]] contained cold and nutrient-rich waters that provided abundant food, ensuring the survival and reproduction of penguins.<ref name="Loughlin-2022" /> Over millions of years, the penguins underwent adaptations, developing unique features that enabled their existence and allowed them to thrive under such an ecological niche.<ref name="Loughlin-2022" /> Today, the Galápagos penguins are among the smallest penguin species.<ref name="Pan-2019">{{Cite journal |last1=Pan |first1=Hailin |last2=Cole |first2=Theresa L |last3=Bi |first3=Xupeng |last4=Fang |first4=Miaoquan |last5=Zhou |first5=Chengran |last6=Yang |first6=Zhengtao |last7=Ksepka |first7=Daniel T |last8=Hart |first8=Tom |last9=Bouzat |first9=Juan L |last10=Argilla |first10=Lisa S |author-link10=Lisa Argilla |last11=Bertelsen |first11=Mads F |last12=Boersma |first12=P Dee |last13=Bost |first13=Charles-André |last14=Cherel |first14=Yves |last15=Dann |first15=Peter |date=2019-09-01 |title=High-coverage genomes to elucidate the evolution of penguins |journal=GigaScience |language=en |volume=8 |issue=9 |doi=10.1093/gigascience/giz117 |issn=2047-217X |pmc=6904868 |pmid=31531675}}</ref>

== Distribution ==
Ninety percent of Galápagos penguins live on [[Fernandina Island]] and the west coast of [[Isabela Island (Galápagos)|Isabela Island]], in the western part of the archipelago, but small populations also occur on [[Santiago Island (Galápagos)|Santiago]], [[Bartolomé Island|Bartolomé]], northern [[Santa Cruz Island (Galápagos)|Santa Cruz]], and [[Floreana Island|Floreana]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Galapagos Penguin Species |url=https://galapagosconservation.org.uk/wildlife/galapagos-penguin/ |access-date=1 October 2020 |website=Galapagos Conservation Trust|language=en-GB}}</ref> The northern tip of Isabela crosses the equator, meaning that some Galápagos penguins live in the [[Northern Hemisphere]], the only penguins to do so.

==Behavior==
[[File:Spheniscus mendiculus Galápagos Ecuador DSC00320 ed ad.jpg|thumb|A colony of Galápagos penguins together on a rock in the Galápagos Islands. One penguin can be seen molting.]]
Galápagos penguins are confined to the [[Galápagos Islands]], foraging in the cool [[Cromwell Current]] during the day and returning to the land at night. They eat small schooling fish, mainly mullet, sardines, pilchards and anchovies,<ref name="Wahlstrom" /> and sometimes [[crustacean]]s. They play a role in regulating the populations of the marine species they consume. At the same time, the adult Galápagos penguins are a food source for other predators such as sharks and whales. They normally range only a few kilometers from their breeding sites, depending on the cold, nutrient-rich currents to bring them food.

Air temperatures in the Galápagos remain in the range {{convert|15-28|C|F}}. During [[El Niño]] seasons, the penguins defer breeding because their food becomes less abundant; this makes the chances of raising offspring successfully unfavorable compared to the chances of dying in the attempt. This was especially detrimental during the [[1982–83 El Niño event|1982-83 El Niño]], where a decline in population of 77% was observed.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=The Climate of the "El Niño" Winter of 1982–83—A Season of Extraordinary Climatic Anomalies|last=Quiroz|first=Roderick S.|date=1983|journal=Monthly Weather Review|volume=111|issue=8|page=1685|doi=10.1175/1520-0493(1983)111<1685:TCOTNW>2.0.CO;2|bibcode=1983MWRv..111.1685Q|issn=1520-0493|doi-access=free}}</ref> The penguins usually breed when the [[sea surface temperature]] is below {{convert|25|C|F|abbr=on}}. The strong tropical sun is problematic for this species. Their primary means of cooling off is going in the water, but other behavioral adaptations for [[thermoregulation]] come into play when they must remain on land. One method involves stretching out their flippers and hunching forward to keep the sun from shining on their feet, which exchange heat rapidly because they have high blood flow and lack insulation. Another method is to pant, using evaporation to cool the throat and airways. Galápagos penguins protect their eggs and chicks from the hot sun by keeping them in deep crevices in the rocks.
[[File:Galapagos Penguin (Spheniscus mendiculus) (16224588607).jpg|thumb|Galápagos penguin swimming in water]]
The Galápagos penguins' flipper-like wings and streamlined bodies enhance their easy movements in water.<ref name="Rohrer-2023">{{Cite journal |last1=Rohrer |first1=Sage D. |last2=Jiménez-Uzcátegui |first2=Gustavo |last3=Parker |first3=Patricia G. |last4=Chubiz |first4=Lon M. |date=2023-04-01 |title=Composition and function of the Galapagos penguin gut microbiome vary with age, location, and a putative bacterial pathogen |journal=Scientific Reports |language=en |volume=13 |issue=1 |page=5358 |doi=10.1038/s41598-023-31826-y |issn=2045-2322 |pmc=10067942 |pmid=37005428|bibcode=2023NatSR..13.5358R }}</ref> Their white and black colors also aid in thermal regulation and camouflaging.<ref name="Rohrer-2023" /> All these features promote and ensure survival in the harsh conditions present in their coastal habitat, hence the symbiotic relationship between the Galápagos penguins and their natural environment. The penguins' breeding patterns depend on finding suitable nesting places within the volcanic landscape. The Galápagos penguins find refuge in the cool and dark lava caves, which provide the appropriate conditions for raising their offspring since they offer protection from the sun, ensuring survival.<ref name="Rohrer-2023" /> This indicates specific behavioral patterns and features that enhance their adaptability in this particular environment.

===Breeding and reproduction===
[[File:Spheniscus mendiculus juvenile.jpg|upright|thumb|Juvenile before it has banding markings]]
Galápagos penguins are a [[Monogamy in animals|monogamous]] species, each pair mating for life.<ref name="Oceana">{{Cite web|title=Galapagos Penguin|url=https://oceana.org/marine-life/seabirds/galapagos-penguin |access-date=2 November 2020 |website=Oceana|language=en}}</ref> There are around 1,000 breeding pairs of Galápagos penguins in the world, and breeding depends on certain climate events. During El Niño, birds may not breed due to these climate conditions.<ref name="Vargas-2007">{{Cite journal |date=2007-06-01 |title=Modelling the effect of El Niño on the persistence of small populations: The Galápagos penguin as a case study |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0006320707000559 |journal=Biological Conservation |language=en-US |volume=137 |issue=1 |pages=138–148 |doi=10.1016/j.biocon.2007.02.005 |issn=0006-3207 |last1=Vargas |first1=F. Hernán |last2=Lacy |first2=Robert C. |last3=Johnson |first3=Paul J. |last4=Steinfurth |first4=Antje |last5=Crawford |first5=Robert J.M. |last6=Dee Boersma |first6=P. |last7=MacDonald |first7=David W. |bibcode=2007BCons.137..138V |url-access=subscription }}</ref> After completing courtship, with rituals including bill dueling, flipper patting, and mutual preening, the pair will build and maintain a nest.<ref name="Wahlstrom"/> Most nests are seen between May and July because both quantity of food and climate conditions are typically most optimal. The nests are made within {{convert|50|m|ft}} of the water on the shore. Adults stay near the breeding area during the year with their mate. It lays one or two eggs in places such as caves and crevices, protected from direct sunlight, which can cause the eggs to overheat.<ref name="Oceana"/> Temperature of surface water also influences reproduction as they prefer temperatures between {{convert|17|and|22|C|F}} to incubate, above {{convert|25|°C|°F|abbr=on}} causes their nests to fail.<ref name="Boersma-1998"/> [[Egg incubation|Incubation]] takes 38–40 days, with both parents incubating.<ref name="MBCS-2017">{{Cite web|date=18 May 2017 |title=Galapagos Penguins|url=https://marinebio.org/species/galapagos-penguins/spheniscus-mendiculus/ |access-date=12 October 2020 |website=MarineBio Conservation Society|language=en-US}}</ref>

One parent will always stay with the eggs or young chicks while the other may be absent for several days to feed.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Galapagos Penguin Facts {{!}} Birds of the Galapagos Islands|url=https://www.nathab.com/know-before-you-go/galapagos-islands/wildlife-guide/birds/galapagos-penguin/ |access-date=12 November 2020 |website=nathab.com|language=en}}</ref> A pair usually rears only one chick. Galápagos penguins will [[Moulting|molt]] before they breed, and are the only penguins to do this twice a year. Molting takes up to 15 days to complete.<ref name="Penguins-World-2013" /> They do this for their own safety, as food availability in the Galápagos is typically unpredictable.<ref name="CES">{{Cite web|title=Galápagos Penguin Biology|url=https://ecosystemsentinels.org/galapagos-penguin-biology/ |access-date=12 October 2020 |website=Center for Ecosystem Sentinels|language=en-US}}</ref> If there is not enough food available, they may abandon the nest.

It takes about 60–65 days for the chicks to become independent.<ref name="MBCS-2017"/> Newly hatched chicks have downy feathers that do not become waterproof until the chicks grow into juveniles.<ref name="CES"/> The juvenile plumage, attained by thirty days after a chick hatches, is dark brown or gray above and white below. These feathers are mainly needed to protect the chicks from the strong sun rather than keep them warm.

Bermudian naturalist [[Louis L. Mowbray]] was the first to successfully breed the Galápagos penguins in captivity.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.bermudabiographies.bm/Bios/bio-mowbray.html|title=Louis Mowbray|access-date=23 July 2011|work=Bermuda Biographies|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110706150457/http://www.bermudabiographies.bm/Bios/bio-mowbray.html|archive-date=6 July 2011}}</ref>

=== Population ===
[[File:Galapagos penguin (Spheniscus mendiculus) -Isabela2.jpg|thumb|255x255px|Adult penguin on Isabela Island]]
Galápagos penguins have a lifespan ranging from 15 to 20 years, but because of environmental factors and predation, their life expectancy is reduced. They are listed on the IUCN Red List of Threatened Species as Endangered.<ref name=iucn/>  Their mean population size from the years 1993–2003 was an average of 1,500.<ref name="Vargas-2007" /> In 2003 there were an estimated 1,351 penguins<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Vargas |first1=HernáN |last2=Lougheed |first2=Cecilia |last3=Snell |first3=Howard |date=April 2005 |title=Population size and trends of the Galápagos Penguin Spheniscus mendiculus |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1474-919x.2005.00412.x |journal=Ibis |language=en |volume=147 |issue=2 |pages=367–374 |doi=10.1111/j.1474-919x.2005.00412.x |issn=0019-1019|url-access=subscription }}</ref> and as of 2018 there are around 1,200 mature penguins left. The Galapagos penguin is currently one of the rarest penguin species,<ref>{{Cite web |last=Peterson |first=Christine |date=2022-10-13 |title=The Galapagos penguin, one of the world's rarest, sees a glimmer of hope |url=https://www.nationalgeographic.com/animals/article/galapagos-penguin-sees-a-glimmer-of-hope |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221013132643/https://www.nationalgeographic.com/animals/article/galapagos-penguin-sees-a-glimmer-of-hope |archive-date=13 October 2022 |access-date=2023-11-15 |website=Animals |language=en}}</ref> next to another penguin species, the [[yellow-eyed penguin]].

The population size on average is about 1,500 individuals per year, depending on the climate events.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Bollmer |first1=Jennifer L. |last2=Vargas |first2=F. Hernán |last3=Parker |first3=Patricia G. |date=July 2007 |title=Low MHC variation in the endangered Galápagos penguin (Spheniscus mendiculus) |journal=Immunogenetics |volume=59 |issue=7 |pages=593–602 |doi=10.1007/s00251-007-0221-y |issn=0093-7711 |pmid=17457582|s2cid=22783121 }}</ref> Population trends were seen to increase and decrease in a span of a year due to [[La Niña]] and [[El Niño]] events, weather events that affect the conditions on the Galápagos Islands. After a La Niña event, both population size and reproduction were both seen to have an increase in number. El Niño events have been found to reduce Galápagos penguin populations, in some cases nearly as much as 50% decrease was seen in population size. This is due to the warmer climate disrupting the cold water that holds the nutrients that support the marine ecosystem.<ref name="Levin-2009">{{Cite journal |last1=Levin |first1=Iris I. |last2=Outlaw |first2=Diana C. |last3=Vargas |first3=F. Hernán |last4=Parker |first4=Patricia G. |date=2009-12-01 |title=Plasmodium blood parasite found in endangered Galapagos penguins (Spheniscus mendiculus) |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0006320709002729 |journal=Biological Conservation |volume=142 |issue=12 |pages=3191–3195 |doi=10.1016/j.biocon.2009.06.017 |bibcode=2009BCons.142.3191L |issn=0006-3207|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

== Conservation ==

=== El Niño and climate change ===
The Galápagos penguin is a particularly vulnerable bird species due to its limited range on the [[Galápagos Islands]]. With a population of only about 1800, it remains on the endangered species list, and its population will likely fluctuate strongly in response to [[mwod:anthropogenic|anthropogenic]] changes in the region.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Galapagos Penguin |url=https://abcbirds.org/bird/galapagos-penguin/ |access-date=2022-03-08 |website=American Bird Conservancy |language=en}}</ref> The primary danger to the Galápagos penguin is the climate phenomena known as [[El Niño]].<ref name="Vargas-2006">{{Cite journal |last1=Vargas |first1=F. Hernán |last2=Harrison |first2=Scott |last3=Rea |first3=Solanda |last4=Macdonald |first4=David W. |date=January 2006 |title=Biological effects of El Niño on the Galápagos penguin |journal=Biological Conservation |volume=127 |issue=1 |pages=107–114 |doi=10.1016/j.biocon.2005.08.001 |bibcode=2006BCons.127..107V |issn=0006-3207}}</ref> In 1982–83 and 1997–98, two strong El Niño events resulted in Galápagos penguin population declines of 77% and 65%, respectively.<ref name="Vargas-2006" /> Additionally, the years 1965–66, 1968–69, 1972–73, 1976, 1986–87, 1991–92 and 1993 all had relatively weak El Niño events which were associated with slow population recovery. Conversely, during [[La Niña]] events when sea surface temperatures are lower than normal and the climate patterns shift, Galápagos penguin populations begin to recover.<ref name="Vargas-2006" /> [[Climate change|Anthropogenic climate change]] has been shown to increase frequency and intensity of El Niño events beyond levels of natural variability which negatively impacts the Galápagos penguin.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Trenberth |first1=Kevin E. |last2=Hoar |first2=Timothy J. |date=1997-12-01 |title=El Niño and climate change |journal=Geophysical Research Letters |volume=24 |issue=23 |pages=3057–3060 |doi=10.1029/97gl03092 |bibcode=1997GeoRL..24.3057T |s2cid=129773511 |issn=0094-8276|doi-access=free }}</ref> Warmer temperatures caused by El Niño are associated with poorer female condition and lower adult body weights. Body conditions fluctuate along with the climate events, evidence showing that weight is enhanced during La Nina's cooler temperatures but body weight deteriorates during El Nino when surface level waters are warmed. Under severe conditions, the penguins can even suffer from starvation.<ref name="Boersma-1998"/> This is due to the warmer temperatures of El Niño events resulting in a decrease in [[upwelling]] of the cold nutrient rich waters which decreases [[phytoplankton]] productivity and results in bottom up [[Trophic level|trophic]] disruptions that reduce the food availability for the Galápagos penguin.<ref name="Boersma-1998">{{Cite journal |last=Boersma |first=P. Dee |date=May 1998 |title=Population Trends of the Galápagos Penguin: Impacts of El Niño and La Niña |journal=The Condor |volume=100 |issue=2 |pages=245–253 |doi=10.2307/1370265 |jstor=1370265 |issn=0010-5422|doi-access=free }}</ref> This lack of food leads to poor breeding success and a disproportionate female death rate, causing population decline and disrupting future recovery by creating uneven sex ratios in the populations.<ref name="Boersma-1998" /> Predictive models suggest future El Niño events will increase in frequency and severity over the next century, posing further threats to the Galápagos penguin. If the pattern of El Niño events from 1965 to 2004 continues, there is a 30% chance of extinction for the Galápagos penguin.<ref name="Vargas-2007"/> If the frequency of strong El Niño events were to double over this same time period, the chance of extinction would be 80%.<ref name="Vargas-2007"/>

=== Disease ===
Another potential threat to the Galápagos penguin is disease. Preliminary studies, such as one conducted in 2001, found no evidence of [[avian malaria]] or [[Marek's disease]] in Galápagos penguin populations.<ref name="Miller-2001">{{Cite journal |last=Miller |first=G.D. |date=2001 |title=Avian Malaria and Marek's disease: Potential threats to Galapagos penguins Spheniscus mendiculus |journal=Marine Ornithology |volume=29 |pages=43–46|doi=10.5038/2074-1235.29.1.490 }}</ref> Despite these findings, the researchers recommended further observations, citing the death of 800 chickens in the Galápagos Islands from Marek's disease, the presence of mosquitos known to carry Avian malaria, and the known impacts of these diseases on other endemic bird populations in [[Hawaii]].<ref name="Miller-2001" /> Later, a 2009 study revealed the presence of a species of ''[[Plasmodium]]'' in Galápagos penguins, a genus of blood parasite that causes Avian malaria.<ref name="Levin-2009"/> The presence of this parasite suggests that diseases are able to travel from other populations to the isolated Galápagos penguin communities and is likely a cause for population decline. Further research suggests that cross-species transmission may occur between [[Endemism|endemic]] Galápagos species and migratory birds such as the [[Bobolink]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=LEVIN |first1=I. I. |last2=ZWIERS |first2=P. |last3=DEEM |first3=S. L. |last4=GEEST |first4=E. A. |last5=HIGASHIGUCHI |first5=J. M. |last6=IEZHOVA |first6=T. A. |last7=JIMÉNEZ-UZCÁTEGUI |first7=G. |last8=KIM |first8=D. H. |last9=MORTON |first9=J. P. |last10=PERLUT |first10=N. G. |last11=RENFREW |first11=R. B. |date=2013-08-23 |title=Multiple Lineages of Avian Malaria Parasites (Plasmodium) in the Galapagos Islands and Evidence for Arrival via Migratory Birds |journal=Conservation Biology |volume=27 |issue=6 |pages=1366–1377 |doi=10.1111/cobi.12127 |pmid=24033638 |bibcode=2013ConBi..27.1366L |s2cid=24300463 |issn=0888-8892}}</ref> Understanding how these diseases reach the Galápagos Islands and transmit between its bird species is a focus for developing conservation strategies for endangered species such as the Galápagos penguin.

=== Other threats ===
Other threats include humans harvesting penguins for oil and other products, competition with fisheries for krill and other fish, habitat loss, increased predation from invasive species, [[bycatch]], and pollution.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Trathan |first1=Phil N. |last2=García-Borboroglu |first2=Pablo |last3=Boersma |first3=Dee |last4=Bost |first4=Charles-André |last5=Crawford |first5=Robert J. M. |last6=Crossin |first6=Glenn T. |last7=Cuthbert |first7=Richard J. |last8=Dann |first8=Peter |last9=Davis |first9=Lloyd Spencer |last10=De La Puente |first10=Santiago |last11=Ellenberg |first11=Ursula |date=2014-08-07 |title=Pollution, habitat loss, fishing, and climate change as critical threats to penguins |journal=Conservation Biology |volume=29 |issue=1 |pages=31–41 |doi=10.1111/cobi.12349 |pmid=25102756 |s2cid=2903491 |issn=0888-8892|doi-access=free |hdl=11336/22668 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> On [[Isabela Island (Galápagos)|Isabela Island]], humans may be contributing to the decline of this species due to introduced cats, dogs, and rats which attack penguins, destroy nests, and spread disease.<ref name="Oceana" /> Other threats on land include [[Grapsus grapsus|Sally lightfoot crab]]s, Galapagos snakes (''[[Pseudalsophis slevini]]'' and ''[[Pseudalsophis dorsalis]]''), [[Oryzomys galapagoensis|Galapagos rice rats]], [[Galapagos hawk|Galápagos hawk]]s, and [[short-eared owl]]s.<ref name="Wahlstrom" /> While in the water, predators include sharks, fur seals, and sea lions.<ref name="MBCS-2017" /><ref>{{cite web |title=The Adaptations of the Galapagos Penguin For a Harsh and Unpredictable Environment |url=http://www.stanford.edu/class/anthsci10sc/2002_galapagos_website/Albert-Penguin.htm |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090529113427/http://www.stanford.edu/class/anthsci10sc/2002_galapagos_website/Albert-Penguin.htm |archive-date=29 May 2009}}</ref> They also face the hazards of unreliable food resources and volcanic activity. [[Illegal, unreported and unregulated fishing|Illegal fishing]] activity may interrupt the penguins' nesting, and they are often caught as bycatch in fishing nets.

These impacts are particularly threatening because of the population structure of the Galápagos penguin.<ref name="Nims-2007">{{Cite journal |last1=Nims |first1=Benjamin D. |last2=Vargas |first2=F. Hernán |last3=Merkel |first3=Jane |last4=Parker |first4=Patricia G. |date=2007-11-21 |title=Low genetic diversity and lack of population structure in the endangered Galápagos penguin (Spheniscus mendiculus) |journal=Conservation Genetics |volume=9 |issue=6 |pages=1413–1420 |doi=10.1007/s10592-007-9465-1 |s2cid=5729128 |issn=1566-0621}}</ref> The Galápagos penguin consists of two geographic subpopulations, but studies suggest that there is sufficient [[gene flow]] between these populations to treat them together when considering conservation strategies.{{Citation needed|date=May 2025}} Additionally, the Galápagos penguin demonstrates relatively low [[genetic diversity]], making it especially vulnerable to disease, predation, and other environmental changes.<ref name="Nims-2007" />

As ecological tourism becomes more common on the Galápagos, it is having a more significant impact on native wildlife, including the Galápagos penguins.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Boersma |first=P. Dee |title=Penguins as Marine Sentinels |journal=BioScience|date=2008 |volume=58 |issue=7 |pages=597–607 |doi=10.1641/B580707 |s2cid=84013421 }}</ref> When incorrectly executed and poorly managed, tourism can contribute to reproductive failure, burrow collapse, or even death to these animals.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Wagner |first1=Eric L. |last2=Borboroglu |first2=Pablo Garcia |last3=Boersma |first3=P. Dee |date=2021-02-15 |title=The power of penguins: Where tourists travel to see penguins in the wild |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0964569120303367 |journal=Ocean & Coastal Management |volume=201 |article-number=105429 |doi=10.1016/j.ocecoaman.2020.105429 |bibcode=2021OCM...20105429W |s2cid=228839494 |issn=0964-5691|url-access=subscription |hdl=11336/136650 |hdl-access=free }}</ref>

Galápagos penguins are protected under the Antarctic Treaty of 1959, which includes all 18 penguin species.<ref>{{Citation |last1=Linton |first1=Heather |title=SeaWorld Parks and Entertainment |date=2019-10-30 |work=Strategic management for hospitality and tourism |pages=253–270 |edition=2nd |location=New York |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-351-18851-7 |last2=Okumus |first2=Fevzi|doi=10.4324/9781351188517-21 |s2cid=214365817 }}</ref> Additionally, Galápagos penguins are protected under Ecuadorian jurisdiction; the [[Galápagos National Park]] and the [[Galápagos Marine Reserve]] are two important entities that are responsible for protecting the Galápagos islands' various species, including the Galápagos penguins.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Jones |first=Peter |date=2019-08-09 |title=A Governance Analysis of the Galápagos Marine Reserve |doi=10.31230/osf.io/ymg9c }}</ref> They were able to accomplish this through limiting human activity and interference from outside factors such as fishing and introducing new species to the island.

== Ecology ==
The Galápagos penguin are unique to the Galápagos Islands, predominantly inhabiting Fernandina Island and Isabela Island. These islands offer an environment crucial for these birds' survival and proliferation.

=== Habitat and interaction with the environment ===
The Galápagos penguins have a symbiotic relationship with their habitat. Fernandina and Isabela Islands provide rocky coastlines ideal for nesting, and the surrounding waters, enriched by the [[Cromwell Current]], abound with fish and crustaceans that form their diet.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Boersma |first=P. Dee |date=2008-07-01 |title=Penguins as Marine Sentinels |journal=BioScience |volume=58 |issue=7 |pages=597–607 |doi=10.1641/b580707 |s2cid=84013421 |issn=1525-3244}}</ref> This feeding habit plays a pivotal role in the local marine ecosystem. They help balance the marine food web and contribute to [[nutrient cycling]]. The [[guano]] produced by these penguins is a rich fertilizer, enhancing the fertility of the coastal soil and indirectly supporting certain plant species native to the islands.<ref name="Vargas-2007"/>{{Failed verification|date=May 2025}}

=== Adaptation to island conditions ===
Adapting to the equatorial climate of the Galápagos, these penguins exhibit unique behavioral and physiological traits. Unlike their Antarctic counterparts, they have evolved to withstand warmer temperatures. They pant and seek shade during the hottest parts of the day to regulate body temperature. Physiologically, they have a reduced layer of body fat and smaller size, aiding heat dissipation.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Fraser |first=W.R. |date=June 1996 |title='''The Penguins'''''T.D. Williams'' With Illustrations by ''J.N. Davies & J. Busby'' Oxford University Press, Oxford (1995). 295 pages. £35.00. ISBN 0 19 854667 x. |journal=Antarctic Science |volume=8 |issue=2 |page=220 |doi=10.1017/s0954102096230299 |s2cid=129247376 |issn=0954-1020}}</ref>{{irrelevant citation|date=May 2025|reason=Book review with no information about penguins in the review.}}

Galápagos penguins breed throughout the year, capitalizing on the fluctuating availability of food resources linked to ocean currents.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Valle |first1=Carlos A. |last2=Coulter |first2=Malcolm C. |date=May 1987 |title=Present Status of the Flightless Cormorant, Galapagos Penguin and Greater Flamingo Populations in the Galapagos Islands, Ecuador, after the 1982-83 El Nino |journal=The Condor |volume=89 |issue=2 |page=276 |doi=10.2307/1368480 |jstor=1368480 |issn=0010-5422}}</ref> This flexibility in breeding patterns is a critical adaptation for survival in an environment where food availability is inconsistent.

=== Impact of environmental factors on life expectancy and population ===
Environmental conditions significantly influence the life expectancy and population numbers of Galápagos penguins. Climate events like [[El Niño]] significantly affect the marine ecosystem, reducing food availability. During such events, penguin populations face starvation, reduced breeding success, and increased mortality.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Vargas |first=F. Hernán |last2=Harrison |first2=Scott |last3=Rea |first3=Solanda |last4=Macdonald |first4=David W. |year=2006 |title=Biological effects of El Niño on the Galápagos penguin |url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S0006320705003095 |journal=Biological Conservation |language=en |volume=127 |issue=1 |pages=107–114 |doi=10.1016/j.biocon.2005.08.001|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Furthermore, human-induced factors such as pollution, oil spills, and fishing activities pose additional threats to their survival.

Conservation efforts are crucial for protecting these penguins, which are classified as endangered by the [[IUCN Red List]].<ref name=iucn /> Measures include monitoring population trends, habitat preservation, and mitigating human impacts. These efforts are essential to ensure the survival of the Galápagos penguin, a species integral to the biodiversity and ecological balance of the Galápagos Islands.

zh.extract:
加岛企鹅（台湾作加拉巴哥環企鵝，学名：Spheniscus mendiculus），又稱加拉帕戈斯企鵝、加島環企鵝，為環企鵝屬的企鵝，分布於南美洲外海的科隆群岛，是唯一一種分布於熱帶，乃至赤道以北的企鵝。由于受到秘鲁寒流和克伦威尔洋流的共同影响，科隆群岛的环境气温远低于赤道其他地区，这才使得企鹅可以在此生存。南非企鹅、南美企鹅和秘鲁企鹅皆其近親。加岛企鹅主要分布在科隆群岛主岛伊莎贝拉岛（Isla Isabela）西岸外的费尔南迪纳岛（Isla Fernandina）上，但群岛中的其它島嶼也有一些小型的加岛企鹅族群分佈。


歷史
加岛企鹅是體型第三小的企鵝，身高約49-50公分，體重約2.5-4.5公斤。牠們是唯一涉足北半球的野生企鵝，表示牠們比大部份野生企鵝生活的地方都要更北。百分之九十的加岛企鹅住在西科隆群岛的伊莎贝拉岛和费尔南迪纳岛上，其餘則居住在聖地亚哥岛、巴托洛梅島、聖克魯斯岛北部及弗洛雷安納島。


参考资料
BirdLife International. Spheniscus mendiculus. The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species 2005.   [11 May 2006] （英语）.  Database entry includes justification for why this species is endangered
